Tafseer:
1. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

Alif, Lam, Meem. (1)
A comprehensive discussion on the disjointed letters (Huroof-e-Muqatta'at) has already passed in the commentary of Surah al-Baqarah.

These are the verses of the wise Book. A guidance and mercy for the doers of good. (2-3)

'These' (Tilka) points to 'Alif, Lam, Meem.' This is the actual Qur'anic name of this Surah. The meaning is that this Surah consists of the verses of a wise Book, but the value of these pearls of wisdom is not appreciated by everyone. Only those who are 'doers of good' (muhsin) will value them. 'Muhsin' refers to those upright people who utilize their faculties of thought and understanding correctly, keep their natural capabilities alive, and take every step in the right direction to the extent of their vision. It states that for such people, these verses are a guidance and mercy. They will derive guidance from them in this world, and in the hereafter, these verses will be a source of grace and mercy for them. The word 'muhsin' has been discussed previously in this book and will appear ahead in verse 22, where it will be further explained. This introduction makes it clear that the neglect of this Book by the deniers is no proof of its lack of value, but is proof of the distorted nature of those ungrateful people. Therefore, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) should not feel disheartened by their conduct.

Those who establish prayer and give zakat, and of the Hereafter they are certain. (4)

This describes the qualities of the doers of good (muhsineen), portraying those who were the true manifestation of this word at that time. It states that these are the people who establish prayer, give zakat, and possess certain faith in the hereafter.

'And of the Hereafter they are certain' (Wa hum bil-akhirati hum yooqinoon) is a high commendation of these believers. It means: in truth, these are the ones who have certain faith in the hereafter. It is this very certainty that has enabled them to establish prayer and give zakat. This shows that those who are unmindful of prayer and zakat are actually devoid of certainty in the hereafter, and if they claim otherwise, they are untruthful in their claim.

Those are on [right] guidance from their Lord, and it is those who are the successful. (5)

It states that these are the ones who are on the straight path of their Lord in this world, and they are the ones who will attain success and salvation in the hereafter. All others are in deviation and will be cast into Hell.

And of the people is he who buys the amusement of speech to mislead [others] from the way of Allah without knowledge and who takes it in amusement. Those will have a humiliating punishment. (6)

'Buys' (yashtaree) can carry the meaning of preferring, as explained in its respective place. 'Amusement of speech' (lahwal-hadeeth) is identical in style to 'adorned speech' (zukhrufal-qawl) used in other places. Here, this term is contrasted with the wise verses of the Book. Therefore, it refers to those misleading arguments and pastimes that the corrupt people of the time spread to turn people away from the verses of Allah. The Qur'an sought to make people face the actual realities of life, but the opponents tried to keep them occupied in these very vanities. The text points to this situation, expressing surprise at their conduct: Allah has revealed a wise Book for their guidance, yet many of them prefer those useless pastimes that validate their desires and innovations.

'To mislead [others] from the way of Allah without knowledge and who takes it in amusement' (Liyudilla 'an sabeelillahi bighayri 'ilmin wa yattakhidhaha huzuwa). 'Knowledge' ('ilm) here means proof or argument, and will appear ahead in verse 30. The meaning is that all the efforts of the corrupters are directed at stopping people from His path, though they have no proof for the path they follow and invite others to. Yet, they possess such arrogance that they mock His verses and use baseless arguments to defend their vanities.

'Those will have a humiliating punishment' (ola'ika lahum 'adhabum-muheen) contrasts their end with that of the believers mentioned above. It states that for those who actively spread deviation against His wise verses, there will be a humiliating punishment. 'Humiliating' (muheen) is specified because showing pride against the truth is arrogance (istikbar), and the punishment for arrogance in His law is humiliation and disgrace. It should be noted that a humiliating punishment is the most severe, as it completely strips away the denier's pride.

And when Our verses are recited to him, he turns away arrogantly as if he had not heard them, as if there was in his ears deafness. So give him glad tidings of a painful punishment. (7)

This depicts the arrogance that makes them deserve a humiliating punishment. It states that when Our verses are recited to him, he turns away in pride as if he had not heard them, as if he were deaf, treating Our verses as unworthy of attention. It states that if their pride has reached the point where they refuse to pay attention to Our words, then announce to them a painful punishment. When a person behaves with arrogance and pride against guidance, his disease is incurable, and such people are destined for Hell.

Indeed, those who believe and do righteous deeds - for them are the Gardens of Pleasure. Wherein they will abide eternally. [It is] the promise of Allah in truth. And He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise. (8-9)

This describes the reward of those who believe in His verses and align their lives accordingly. It states that for them are the Gardens of Pleasure, wherein they will abide eternally. 'The promise of Allah in truth' (wa'dallahi haqqan) is His definitive promise that will surely be fulfilled. This phrase carries high emphasis, which was necessary because verse 6 mentioned that the arrogant mock His verses. Their mockery was specifically directed at the verses promising an eternal kingdom to the poor, weak believers of that time. Therefore, the text states with full emphasis: 'Let them mock if they will, but the believers should rest assured that Allah's promise is true.'

'And He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise' (Wa Huwal-Azizul-Hakeem) provides the proof of His promise through His attributes. If Allah were not to reward the believers and punish the wrongdoers, it would mean He is neither Mighty nor Wise, but completely helpless, having created this universe in vain. This is obviously false; He is indeed Mighty and Wise, and His law of recompense is absolute.

He created the heavens without pillars that you see, and has cast into the earth firm mountains lest it should shift with you, and dispersed therein from every creature. And We sent down rain from the sky, causing to grow therein of every noble kind. (10)

This points to the signs of the universe, demonstrating to any rational person that the Creator is both All-Mighty (dominant over all things) and All-Wise (His every creation displaying His wisdom). He established the heavens without visible pillars, placed mountains on earth to keep it stable, spread various creatures on it, sent down rain from the sky, and caused beneficial things to grow.

'Without pillars that you see' (bighayri 'amadin tarawnaha) means He established the heavens on pillars that are invisible, binding the universe with the laws of gravity and attraction known to Him alone.

'Of every noble kind' (min kulli zawjin kareem). 'Kareem' literally means beneficial, pointing to His mercy and rububiyyah: He established this grand palace and filled it with boundless blessings for the sustenance of its dwellers.

This is the creation of Allah. So show Me what those other than Him have created. Rather, the wrongdoers are in clear error. (11)

Meaning, since all these things are created by Allah—and you admit they are His creation—show Me what your false deities have created, that you have associated them with His divinity and rights!

'Rather, the wrongdoers are in clear error' (baliz-zalimoona fee dalalim-mubeen) is addressed turning away from them, expressing sorrow over their stubbornness: they have no proof to defend their polytheism, yet they persist in this clear deviation.

2. The upcoming subject... Verses 12-19

Next is the reference to Luqman's wisdom and his wise advice to his son. This is intended to present to the Arabs their own respected sages, showing them that those blessed with His wisdom also taught the same principles that this wise Book is inviting them to. However, they are so occupied with the blind following of ancestral traditions that they ignore the wisdom of their own ancestors. Consequently, while Luqman taught these principles to his son with deep concern, they are punishing their own children for accepting these very principles today.

Who was Luqman? Some have suggested he was of Nubian origin, but we do not find this correct. Historians like Ibn Ishaq traced his lineage to Abraham (peace be upon him), but this is also uncertain. Ancient Arab poets like Tarafah ibn al-Abd and Salma ibn Rabi'ah mentioned Luqman and his tribe, indicating that he belonged to the remnant of the nation of 'Ad in Yemen, where they enjoyed great status.

Some have regarded him as a prophet, but the majority of scholars hold that he was a wise sage, not a prophet, as is supported by Arab literature and the Qur'an. His advice to his son is also mentioned in ancient Arab poetry. Some historians regarded him as a king, but even if he was not a king, he held the status of a respected chief among his people, and his advice was likely delivered when he was preparing his son for leadership responsibilities. Read the upcoming verses in this light.

3. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

And We had certainly given Luqman wisdom [and said], "Be grateful to Allah." And whoever is grateful is grateful only for [the benefit of] himself. And whoever is ungrateful - then indeed, Allah is Free of need and Praiseworthy. (12)

The first fruit of wisdom is gratitude to Allah. When Allah blesses a servant with wisdom, the first effect is that he becomes a grateful servant of his Lord, which is the foundation of recognizing all His rights and the rights of people. We have discussed this in detail in Surah al-Fatihah. This has also been alluded to in the accounts of David, Solomon, and Dhul-Qarnayn. This shows that the first sign of a wise person is his gratitude to his Lord; without it, his claims to knowledge and philosophy are hollow.

'And whoever is grateful is grateful only for [the benefit of] himself. And whoever is ungrateful - then indeed, Allah is Free of need and Praiseworthy' (Wa man yashkur fa-innama yashkuru li-nafsih, wa man kafara fa-innallaha Ghaniyyun Hameed). This is a reassuring clarification: let none assume that Allah's demand for gratitude serves any need of His; rather, He has ordained it for the benefit of His servants. A grateful servant increases His blessings upon himself, while Allah is Free of need (Ghani)—He is neither benefited by gratitude nor harmed by ingratitude—and He is Praiseworthy (Hameed), possessing all perfect attributes in His Essence, continuing to provide for His creation regardless of their gratitude or ingratitude. This will be further explained in verse 26.

And [remember] when Luqman said to his son while he was advising him, "O my son, do not associate [anything] with Allah. Indeed, association [with Him] is a great injustice." (13)

Just as the first fruit of wisdom is gratitude to Allah, the core of gratitude is avoiding polytheism. Since all blessings are from Allah, the servant must attribute them to Him alone. If he associates partners with Him, he is guilty of violating the greatest right, which is indeed a great injustice (zulmun 'azeem).

It is noteworthy that Luqman delivered these words as 'advice' (ya'idhuhu) to his son, which shows that this was not a casual statement, but was delivered with special care and emphasis on an important occasion. This is related to warn the Quraysh: 'A wise father gave this advice to his son, yet you are doing the opposite, punishing your children for accepting it!'

And We have enjoined upon man care for his parents. His mother carried him, [increasing her] in weakness upon weakness, and his weaning is in two years. Be grateful to Me and to your parents; to Me is the destination. And if they strive to make you associate with Me that of which you have no knowledge, do not obey them but accompany them in [this] world with appropriate kindness and follow the way of those who turn back to Me. Then to Me is your return, and I will inform you of what you used to do. (14-15)

These two verses are a parenthetical addition by Allah within the advice of Luqman. Luqman instructed his son to be grateful to Allah, but out of humility, he refrained from mentioning his own rights alongside the rights of Allah, and proceeded to the mention of the Resurrection. Allah filled this gap by mentioning the rights of parents. He states: 'We have enjoined upon man care for his parents.' We have explained that the duty of care toward parents is a fundamental teaching of all scriptures. This is detailed as: 'Be grateful to Me and to your parents' (Anishkur lee wa li-walidayk)—coupling His gratitude with the gratitude of parents. This shows that parents possess the greatest right after the right of Allah, as they are the means of his creation and care, but this is a right of care and service, not worship.

'But if they strive to make you associate with Me that of which you have no knowledge, do not obey them but accompany them in [this] world with appropriate kindness' (Wa in jahadaka 'ala an tushrika bee ma laysa laka bihi 'ilmun fala tuti'huma wa sahibhuma fid-dunya ma'roofa). If parents pressure their children to associate partners with Allah without any proof, they have no right to be obeyed, and the children must refuse. Polytheism is described as 'without knowledge' because there is no proof to defend it, while the existence of God is an obvious reality. However, while refusing to obey them in polytheism, the children must accompany them in this world with appropriate kindness, providing for their needs and praying for their guidance. Abraham (peace be upon him) prayed for his father until he was stopped. Complete dissociation from disbelieving parents is valid only when the Muslim community formally declares dissociation from their nation, as explained in Surah al-Bara'ah.

It is noteworthy that while care and gratitude are enjoined for both parents, the sacrifices of the mother are specifically highlighted: 'His mother carried him, [increasing her] in weakness upon weakness, and his weaning is in two years' (hamalathu ummuhu wahnan 'ala wahnin wa fisaluhu fee 'amayn). This refers to pregnancy, delivery, and nursing, all of which are borne by the mother, which is why her right is greater than the father's, as is supported by the famous Hadith.

'To Me is the destination' (ilayyal-maseer) is a warning: everyone must return to Me, and none can escape His accountability for violating His rights or the rights of parents.

'And follow the way of those who turn back to Me' (wattabi' sabeela man anaba ilayh) is a further instruction: the believer must follow the path of those who turn to Him, not those who deviate, even if they are his parents. In Surah al-Ankabut, the disbelievers' words are quoted: 'Follow our way, and we will carry your sins' (verse 12). This verse refutes their claim: 'None has the right to invite to a path contrary to His way; follow only those who are on His path.'

'Then to Me is your return, and I will inform you of what you used to do' is addressed to both parents and children, carrying a warning and reassurance: 'I will call everyone to account. If parents misuse their authority to divert their children from My path, they will face punishment, and if the children stand firm on My path, they will be rewarded.'

[Luqman said], "O my son, indeed, if there should be the weight of a mustard seed and should be within a rock or in the heavens or in the earth, Allah will bring it forth. Indeed, Allah is Subtle and Aware." (16)

The parenthetical addition concludes, and Luqman's advice resumes. After teaching monotheism, he addressed the second fundamental belief—the Resurrection. He warned his son: 'O my son, if any deed of yours, good or bad, is as tiny as a mustard seed, even if it is hidden inside a rock, or in the vast heavens, or in the depths of the earth, Allah will bring it forth, for He is Subtle and Aware.'

It should be noted that ignorance of His all-encompassing knowledge led past nations to polytheism and deviation regarding the afterlife. Therefore, Luqman presented this wisdom to make his son's mind clear regarding both monotheism and the Resurrection.

'Indeed, if there should be...' (Innahu in taku...). The pronoun is used for emphasis, referring to the deed as tiny as a mustard seed.

'A rock' (sakhratin) can refer to a mountain or a ravine, which is linguistically correct in Arabic.

"O my son, establish prayer, enjoin what is right, forbid what is wrong, and be patient over what befalls you. Indeed, [all] that is of the matters [requiring] determination." (17)

After teaching gratitude, he instructed his son on how to display this gratitude in practical life. First, he mentioned prayer, which is the primary expression of His gratitude, as taught by all prophets. He said: 'O my son, establish prayer' (aqimis-salat), meaning observe and maintain it with all its rights.

'Enjoin what is right' (wa'mur bil-ma'roof) connects the servant with creation. While prayer connects him with his Lord, enjoining right connects him with His servants. 'Right' (ma'roof) includes all virtuous deeds, such as charity, helping the needy, and other good deeds recognized by any righteous society.

'And forbid what is wrong' (wanha 'anil-munkar) is the negative aspect, enjoining him to restrain people from miserliness, injustice, and other wrong deeds.

'And be patient over what befalls you' (wasbir 'ala ma asabak) warnings him that enjoining right and forbidding wrong is not an easy path; it is filled with difficulties and opposition, and one cannot travel it without patience.

'Indeed, [all] that is of the matters [requiring] determination' (inna dhalika min 'azmil-umoor) emphasizes that these tasks require firm determination, and can only be performed by those who commit themselves to His cause.

"And do not turn your cheek [in contempt] toward people and do not walk through the earth exultantly. Indeed, Allah does not love the self-deluded and boastful." (18)

'Turning one's cheek' (tusa'ir khaddaka) means to turn away from people in pride.

'Toward people' (lin-nas) refers to people in general, particularly the poor and weak whom the wealthy treat with contempt.

After detailing the expressions of gratitude, he warned against what contradicts gratitude. The expression of gratitude is humility, and its opposite is pride. Short-sighted people turn arrogant when blessed, treating others with contempt. Luqman warned his son against this conduct.

'And do not walk through the earth exultantly' (wa la tamshi fil-ardi maraha) is identical to the verse in Surah al-Isra (verse 37), which adds: 'Indeed, you will never tear the earth [apart], and you will never reach the mountains in height.' Since His majesty is obvious, what is the basis for human pride? Pride is displayed in one's expression, posture, and gait; Luqman warned his son against both displays.

'Indeed, Allah does not love the self-deluded and boastful' (innallaha la yuhibbu kulla mukhtalin fakhoor) is a severe warning: He does not love those who treat His blessings as their own achievements and turn arrogant.

"And be moderate in your pace and lower your voice. Indeed, the most disagreeable of sounds is the voice of donkeys." (19)

The previous warnings were negative, and now he presents the positive aspect of humility: maintain a moderate pace and lower your voice. The preposition 'of' (min) indicates that since Allah has endowed humans with the ability to regulate their voice, they should do so in accordance with the situation, rather than shouting constantly like a donkey. 'Indeed, the most disagreeable of sounds is the voice of donkeys' (inna ankaral-aswati la-sawtul-hameer) is to create aversion to harsh, loud speech: since Allah has blessed humans with the gift of elegant speech, why should they resemble donkeys!

While these wise counsels are beneficial for everyone, they are of ultimate importance for leaders and rulers. This supports our view that Luqman was a wise ruler who was preparing his son for leadership responsibilities. He resembles Prophet David (peace be upon him) in this regard.

4. The upcoming subject... Verses 20-34

Luqman's advice focused primarily on gratitude to Allah. Now, the text details why He alone deserves gratitude, presenting arguments from the universe and human nature. Along with this, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is commanded to remain firm and patient: 'Leave the matter of the opponents to Us; We will judge them. Their demands for signs are empty; His signs are so boundless that if all the trees on earth were pens and the seas were ink—replenished by seven more seas—His signs could not be written.' These are ungrateful and deceitful people who call upon Him in distress but return to polytheism once saved; no sign can benefit them. Finally, humans are warned to fear the Day when no father can benefit his son, nor can a son benefit his father. If the exact time of the Resurrection is unknown, it does not mean it is an imaginary threat, for there are many obvious realities whose exact time is unknown. Read the upcoming verses in this light.

5. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

Do you not see that Allah has subjected to you whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth and amply bestowed upon you His favors, apparent and hidden? But of the people is he who disputes about Allah without knowledge or guidance or a clarifying Book. (20)

The introduction of this Surah concluded in verse 11 with: 'This is the creation of Allah. So show Me what those other than Him have created. Rather, the wrongdoers are in clear error.' Following the reference to Luqman's wisdom, the text returns to the arguments from the universe, expressing surprise at their conduct: 'Do you not see that Allah has subjected to you whatever is in the heavens and the earth, and you cannot claim any other partner in their creation? On what basis then do you worship others?'

'And amply bestowed upon you His favors, apparent and hidden' (Wa asbagha 'alaykum ni'amahu zahiratan-wa batinah). 'Asbagha' means to bestow amply and complete. He has completed His favors upon you in every aspect—both physical, material favors (sustenance, health) and intellectual, spiritual favors (intellect, guidance). None can claim these blessings are from any other than Him, so He alone deserves your gratitude.

'But of the people is he who disputes about Allah without knowledge or guidance or a clarifying Book' (Wa minan-nasi man yujadilu fillahi bighayri 'ilmin wa la hudan wa la Kitabim-muneer). While His rights are obvious, some fools dispute about His monotheism without any proof, guidance, or clarifying Book. 'Knowledge' ('ilm) here means proof or argument, contrasted with mere conjecture. 'Guidance' refers to the teachings of the prophets, and 'clarifying Book' to the revealed scriptures (Torah, Gospel, Qur'an). This is to show that they have no basis for their polytheism, as will be clear ahead.

And when it is said to them, "Follow what Allah has revealed," they say, "Rather, we will follow that upon which we found our fathers." Even if Satan was inviting them to the punishment of the Blaze? (21)

This explains their dispute without knowledge: when they are called to follow the Book revealed by Allah, they respond: 'We will follow only the way of our forefathers.' Ask them: 'Will you persist in this blind following even if Satan is inviting you to the punishment of the Blaze through this prejudice?' Merely being the way of ancestors is no proof of truth; they might have followed others blindly. Sincere people must evaluate their ancestral traditions in the light of His Book, rather than falling into Hell through blind prejudice. The pronoun in 'inviting them' (yad'oohum) refers to the deniers themselves.

And whoever submits his face to Allah while he is a doer of good - then he has grasped the most trustworthy handhold. And to Allah belongs the outcome of [all] matters. (22)

'Submits' (yuslim) is conjoined to 'to Allah' (ilallah), which is used here to denote turning toward Him with complete devotion and sincerity, as is qualified by 'while he is a doer of good' (wa huwa muhsin). 'Ahsan' means to perform an action with ultimate beauty and sincerity, which clarifies that this turning must be with complete focus and devotion—not like the polytheists who claim His name but direct their actual devotion to others.

This response is to those who insist on blind following: ancestral traditions and false deities will not benefit you, but those who turn to Allah with complete sincerity have grasped a most trustworthy handhold that will never break.

'And to Allah belongs the outcome of [all] matters' (Wa ilallahi 'aqibatul-umoor) is a severe warning: let none assume that other handholds will benefit them; since all matters ultimately return to Him alone, no other support will be of any benefit.

And whoever disbelieves - let not his disbelief grieve you. To Us is their return, and We will inform them of what they did. Indeed, Allah is Knowing of that within the breasts. (23)

This consoles the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): do not let their disbelief grieve you, for their ultimate return is to Us alone, and We will call them to account for what they did.

'Indeed, Allah is Knowing of that within the breasts' (innallaha 'aleemun bi-dhatis-sudoor) is a warning: He is fully aware of all secrets, so nothing can escape His accountability.

We grant them enjoyment for a little; then We will force them to a massive punishment. (24)

'Force' (nadtarruhum) followed by the preposition 'to' (ila) indicates dragging them in custody. The respite granted to them in this world is temporary; We will allow them to enjoy themselves for a short time, and then We will drag them to a massive punishment. Human beings are bound by His physical and moral laws on earth, and none can escape His grasp.

And if you ask them, "Who created the heavens and the earth?" they will surely say, "Allah." Say, "[All] praise is [due] to Allah." But most of them do not know. (25)

This is the proof of gratitude, admitted by the polytheists themselves: if you ask them who created the heavens and the earth, they will admit that it is Allah. Say: 'Praise is due to Allah!' Since He is the sole Creator, He alone deserves your gratitude; on what basis then do you associate others with Him, when they have no share in His creation!

'But most of them do not know' (bal aktharuhum la ya'lamoon) means they do not understand the logical requirements of their own admissions, and contradict them through their actions.

To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth. Indeed, Allah - He is the Free of need, the Praiseworthy. (26)

This states the actual reality: everything in the heavens and the earth is His creation and is in need of Him, while He alone is Free of need (Ghani) and Praiseworthy (Hameed); therefore, He alone deserves your gratitude.

And if whatever trees on earth were pens and the sea [were ink], replenished after it by seven [more] seas, the words of Allah would not be exhausted. Indeed, Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise. (27)

This same theme appeared in Surah al-Kahf:

'Say: "If the sea were ink for [writing] the words of my Lord, the sea would be exhausted before the words of my Lord were exhausted, even if We brought the like of it as a supplement."' (al-Kahf: 109)

'Words' (kalimat) refers to His signs that testify to His power and wisdom. Since every sign is a manifestation of His word 'Kun' (Be) and testifies to Him, His signs are called 'words,' which is highly significant. God is not a hidden or obscure entity; His signs are so boundless that if all trees were pens and the seas were ink, they could not be written, and all these signs testify that He is Mighty and Wise—the two attributes that substantiate monotheism, the Resurrection, and messengership.

Linguistically, the matching word 'ink' (midad) is omitted after 'sea,' but has been clarified in the translation.

As explained under Surah al-Kahf, this is a statement of fact: if the sea were ink, it would not be sufficient to write even the wonders of the sea itself, let alone the entire universe. What humans have discovered of this earth is but a tiny drop, and His signs are boundless.

Your creation and your resurrection are not but as a single soul. Indeed, Allah is Hearing and Seeing. (28)

This summarizes the previous arguments: since He is Mighty and Wise, He will surely repeat creation. For the All-Mighty, resurrecting everyone is as simple as resurrecting a single soul; a single word 'Kun' or a single blow of the Horn will bring everyone back to life.

'Indeed, Allah is Hearing and Seeing' (innallaha Samee'un Baseer) is a reminder of His all-encompassing knowledge: He is fully aware of all your deeds, so He will call everyone to account without any difficulty.

Do you not see that Allah causes the night to enter the day and causes the day to enter the night and has subjected the sun and the moon, each running [its course] for a specified term, and that Allah, of what you do, is Aware? (29)

While verse 20 used the plural 'Do you not see' (Alawatam yaraw), here the singular 'Do you not see' (Alam tara) is used to address each individual directly, which carries greater emphasis.

It states that He causes the night to enter the day and the day to enter the night, depicting the consistent alternation of night and day under His command.

'And has subjected the sun and the moon, each running [its course] for a specified term' means He has subjected the sun and the moon to serve His creation, and they run their course with absolute precision, without any deviation.

These signs prove that He can repeat creation and that He alone deserves your worship. It also implies: 'Since He runs this vast system, He is fully aware of your deeds, and He will call you to account.'

That is because Allah is the Truth, and that what they call upon besides Him is falsehood, and because Allah is the Most High, the Grand. (30)

Meaning, this cosmic harmony exists because He alone is the true God (al-Haqq). If there were other gods, this system would have collapsed: 'Had there been therein gods besides Allah, they both [heaven and earth] would have been ruined.' Therefore, all other deities are completely false (al-batil). 'And because Allah is the Most High, the Grand' (Wa annallaha Huwal-'Aliyyul-Kabeer) means He is so High and Grand that associating partners with Him is contrary to His glory.

Do you not see that ships sail through the sea by the favor of Allah that He may show you of His signs? Indeed in that are signs for everyone patient and grateful. (31)

Using the parable of a ship, the text warns: do not turn arrogant in ease or despair in trial, but show patience and gratitude under all circumstances. This is the only path to success, but you enjoy His favor and turn arrogant, only to panic when afflicted, and then return to your ingratitude. For those who wish to learn, there is a great lesson in the state of a ship.

And when waves cover them like canopies, they supplicate Allah, sincere to Him in religion. But when He delivers them to the land, of them are those who are moderate. And none rejects Our signs except everyone ungrateful and treacherous. (32)

This details the parable of the ship: when waves cover them like canopies, they call upon Allah alone with complete sincerity. But when He delivers them safely to the land, only a few remain firm and moderate on the path of truth, while the rest turn ungrateful and treacherous.

This is a warning to the Quraysh: today, they enjoy ease and ignore His warnings, but when His grasp reaches them, they will cry in vain, only to return to their ingratitude once saved. Such ungrateful people do not benefit from His signs.

'Treacherous' (khattar) denotes extreme bad faith and covenant-breaking. 'Sabar' (patient) refers to those who remain firm on their covenant under all circumstances, while 'khattar' refers to those who turn ungrateful once saved—the worst form of bad faith. Such people benefit from neither His favors nor His warnings.

O mankind, fear your Lord and fear a Day when no father will avail his son, nor will a child avail his father at all. Indeed, the promise of Allah is truth, so let not the worldly life delude you and let not the Deceiver delude you about Allah. (33)

This is a final warning: fear your Lord and fear the Day when no father can benefit his son, nor can a son benefit his father. Since the bond between father and son is the closest, and they cannot benefit each other, none other can do so.

Linguistically, the negation regarding the son's inability to benefit his father is phrased with higher emphasis to show that despite his youth, strength, and desire to help, even the son cannot benefit his father on that Day. This refutes the claims of those disbelieving elders who told their juniors: 'Follow our way, and we will carry your sins' (al-Ankabut: 12).

'Indeed, the promise of Allah is truth, so let not the worldly life delude you' means His promise of Resurrection is true, so let not the temporary prosperity of this life delude you into thinking that there is no Resurrection because the wrongdoers are granted respite here. This world is a place of trial, and the final results will appear in the hereafter.

'And let not the Deceiver delude you about Allah' warnings against being deceived regarding His mercy, as if He created this world without any purpose and will not call anyone to account. Those who harbor such false hopes will face His justice on that Day. 'Deceiver' (gharoor) refers to the false illusions of this world and to Satan who deceives humans.

Indeed, Allah [alone] has knowledge of the Hour and sends down the rain and knows what is in the wombs. And no soul perceives what it will earn tomorrow, and no soul perceives in what land it will die. Indeed, Allah is Knowing and Aware. (34)

This is a response to those who mockingly asked when the Resurrection would occur. It states that its exact time is known to Allah alone, but its obscurity is no proof of its non-existence. There are many obvious realities whose exact time is unknown, yet none denies them. Rain is a reality, yet none can predict its exact time or quantity. A mother's pregnancy is a reality, yet none knows what is in the womb except Him. No soul knows what it will earn tomorrow, and no soul knows where it will die; a person may set out in joy and his body is returned in grief. Since you are ignorant of these obvious daily realities of your own lives, why do you doubt the Resurrection because its exact time is unknown!

'Indeed, Allah is Knowing and Aware' (Innahu 'Aleemun Khabeer) means He alone is the All-Knowing, All-Aware, who will surely establish the Resurrection at the appointed time.
